game_name: Romance of the Three Kingdoms II

element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player chooses from 6 scenarios spanning AD 189-220, establishing initial power distribution

element_introduction_order.element: Warlord selection and custom character creation
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player selects which warlord(s) to control; can insert custom characters into unoccupied territories
Stats range 10-100 and influence success in dueling, land value increases, and other tasks
Core objective—accumulate control of all provinces in ancient China
Reputation system affects rates of officer loyalty to lords
Advisers predict success chances of plans; Intelligence stat 100 = perfect prediction
Items increase officer stats; new feature in this iteration
Advanced offensive mechanic for disrupting enemy communications
War Ability stat influences success; used for resolving character confrontations

typical_relief_triggers: Adviser confirmation of successful plan execution
typical_tension_triggers: Multiple warlords competing for same territory
Officer loyalty fluctuation due to reputation
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based strategy with tension rising from multi-faction competition and character stat management. Relief comes from successful campaign progress and adviser validation of plans. Pacing controlled by player with no time-pressure mechanics.

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Custom character placement in unoccupied territories represents player agency and optional expansion

difficulty_curve.early_game: Low friction—player selects starting conditions via 6 historical scenarios; can customize character roster before campaign begins
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Escalating complexity—reputation system, adviser consultation, multiple officers with loyalty fluctuation require strategic planning
difficulty_curve.late_game: Resource consolidation—controlling 30+ provinces simultaneously while managing officer stats and executing coordinated plans
notable_spikes: Transition from scenario 1 (Dong Zhuo seizes Luoyang, single dominant power) to scenario 6 (Rise of Wei/Wu/Shu, three-way division)—multiplayer chaos increases
Introduction of messenger interception—new offensive tactical layer

notable_level_patterns.description: 6 scenarios represent different historical moments in the Three Kingdoms period. Each has distinct initial warlord positions and power distribution, affecting early-game difficulty and available alliances. No fixed progression—player chooses entry point.

41 provinces form a connected map. Controlling adjacent territories facilitates expansion. Victory requires 100% province control. Early phases favor partial consolidation; late phases demand managing borders against multi-front pressure.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Scenario 4 (Cao Cao supremacy) involves managing northern expansion while defending against Wu and Shu

notable_level_patterns.description: Characters begin with fixed stats; treasures/items incrementally increase them. Higher stats unlock more successful plan execution. Late-game officers become nearly guaranteed successes with sufficient stat investment.
notable_level_patterns.example_level: Recruitment → Item gifting → High-difficulty task execution
